bullets.text: Progressive reported its first quarterly net loss since Q2 2000 and a year-to-date net loss of $229.3 million, driven by over $1 billion in other-than-temporary impairment losses on preferred stocks in financial institutions.

Management outlined a three-layer capital framework: regulatory capital (~$5B at 3:1 premium-to-surplus), an extreme contingency reserve of several hundred million, and an excess layer; capital never fell below layers one and two.
Underwriting combined ratio was 95.1 for Q3, including ~$62 million (~1.8 points) from Hurricanes Gustav and Ike; insurance operations were described as acceptable despite the investment losses.

Q3 marked the first quarter-over-prior-year written premium growth since Q3 2006 at just under 1%, as rising average premiums and retention-driven unit growth began contributing to top-line improvement.

Claims frequency declined high single-digit percentages in primary coverages throughout Q3; management pledged continued pricing agility given falling gas prices and uncertain long-term frequency trends.
Progressive handled approximately 13,000 claims from Gustav and Ike; closure rates were slower than prior storms due to power outages and destruction, but were substantially complete by mid-October.
Agency auto remained challenged at the unit level; Direct auto showed early acceleration mostly via Internet sales, with several important states receiving rate or program approvals.

Rate changes are starting to earn in, leading to increasing average premiums; management expects rate activity to continue, noting that reduced certainty from gas price swings requires continued pricing agility.
Special Lines posted a good volume year; Commercial Auto inherently more vulnerable to economic slowdown with declining new business applications and vehicles listed on in-force policies.
